title: Your first typed function
instruction:
A function with typed parameters and a return type is a **contract**: TypeScript checks every caller and every return statement against it.

Write a function that calculates compound interest.
successMessage: The function signature `(principal: number, rate: number, years: number): number` tells TypeScript — and every developer reading this code — exactly what goes in and what comes out. No documentation needed.

title: Arrow functions — concise syntax
instruction:
Arrow functions with type annotations are the standard for callbacks, transformations, and one-liners. The return type can often be inferred.

Rewrite the same calculation as an arrow function.
successMessage: Arrow functions are concise and work seamlessly with array methods like `.map()`. TypeScript infers the return type from the expression — no need to annotate `formatCurrency`'s return as `string`.

title: Default parameters — avoid undefined
instruction:
Default parameters provide fallback values when a caller doesn't pass an argument (or passes `undefined`). They're better than `||` for this purpose.

Build a pagination helper with sensible defaults.
successMessage: Default parameters make functions self-documenting and safe to call with partial arguments. The return type is inferred from the object literal — TypeScript knows exactly what `paginate()` returns.

title: Optional parameters — may or may not be provided
instruction:
Mark a parameter with `?` to make it optional. Inside the function, TypeScript knows it may be `undefined` and enforces you handle that case.

Build a logging function where context is optional.
successMessage: Optional parameters are `T | undefined` inside the function. TypeScript enforces you check `userId` before using it as a number. The `?` in the signature communicates intent to callers too.

title: Rest parameters — variable argument lists
instruction:
Rest parameters collect any number of arguments into a typed array. They must be the last parameter.

Build a stats function that accepts any number of scores.
successMessage: Rest parameters collect all arguments into `number[]`. The caller can pass 1 argument or 100. TypeScript ensures every argument is a `number` — `stats(88, "ninety")` would error.

title: Function types — functions as values
instruction:
In TypeScript, functions are first-class values. You can describe a function's shape with a type, then pass functions around just like any other value.

Build a pipeline that applies multiple text transformations.
successMessage: `TextTransformer` is a function type. `addPrefix` returns a `TextTransformer` — a function that creates functions. This is the foundation of functional programming patterns in TypeScript.

title: Void return type — side-effect functions
instruction:
`void` means the function doesn't return a meaningful value. It's used for functions whose purpose is a side effect: logging, sending an event, updating state.

Build an event emitter pattern.
successMessage: `void` explicitly signals this function is for side effects. Callers know not to use the return value. The `EventHandler` type makes every handler consistent — any deviation is a type error.

title: Overloads — multiple function signatures
instruction:
Sometimes a function behaves differently depending on its argument types. Function overloads let you define multiple signatures and TypeScript picks the right one.

Build a `format` function that formats differently based on the input type.
successMessage: Overload signatures appear before the implementation signature. TypeScript uses the first matching overload — callers see clean, specific signatures, not the messy union type of the implementation.

title: Higher-order functions — functions that return functions
instruction:
A higher-order function either takes functions as parameters or returns a function. This pattern is everywhere in TypeScript — middleware, decorators, memoization.

Build a rate limiter using a higher-order function.
successMessage: `rateLimit` wraps any function and enforces a time gap between calls. The generic `T extends unknown[]` captures the parameter types of the wrapped function — so `throttledAlert` still expects a `string`.

title: Memoization — caching function results
instruction:
Memoization caches function results for expensive computations. It's a classic higher-order function pattern.

Build a generic memoize function.
successMessage: `memoize` works with any function. The generic `TArgs` and `TReturn` capture the wrapped function's exact types — `fastFib` still expects a `number` and returns a `number`.

title: Callbacks with typed parameters
instruction:
Array methods like `.map()`, `.filter()`, and `.reduce()` accept callbacks. When the array is typed, TypeScript knows the callback parameter type automatically.

Process a list of orders using typed callbacks.
successMessage: TypeScript infers the callback parameter type from the array — `o` is `Order` inside `.filter()`, `.map()`, and `.reduce()`. No annotations needed in the callbacks themselves.

title: Function composition
instruction:
Function composition chains multiple functions together so the output of one becomes the input of the next. It's a powerful pattern for building pipelines.

Compose data transformation functions.
successMessage: `compose` chains three functions with precise types: `(A → B) → (B → C) → (C → D) → (A → D)`. TypeScript ensures the types line up — you can't compose `(number → string)` followed by `(number → number)` because the types don't match.

title: Async functions — returning Promises
instruction:
An `async` function always returns `Promise<T>`. TypeScript tracks the type inside the Promise so you know exactly what `await` will give you.

Write async functions that fetch and process data.
successMessage: Inside `main`, `await fetchPrice(plan)` unwraps `Promise<number>` to `number`. TypeScript knows this — `price` is typed as `number`, so `$${price}/mo` works perfectly without any casting.

title: never return type — exhaustive checks
instruction:
`never` is the type for values that should never occur. It's used in exhaustive switch statements to make TypeScript error if a new case is added but not handled.

Build a shape renderer with exhaustive type checking.
successMessage: If you add a new shape to the union but forget to add its `case`, TypeScript errors on the `default: assertNever(shape)` line — because `shape` would no longer be `never`. It's a compile-time guarantee of exhaustiveness.
